params.description: Scale Preview System showing Warmup data with all four scales displaying checkmarks (all scales work).

steps.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!
But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System showing Warmup data with all four scales displaying checkmarks.

params.description: Data table appears showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.
Scale Preview System with Scale of 5 pre-selected. Vertical graph visible with axis 0-50, currently empty.

steps.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most—80 books!
Let me try Scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Preview generates with Scale of 5. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars appear normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the current graph boundary at 50. Warning indicator appears.

steps.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.
I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see... I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80...

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear sequentially: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now shows 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Graph looks crowded with 17 tick marks total.

steps.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20... all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks!

params.description: All 17 tick marks on the axis are visually emphasized to show how crowded the graph is.

steps.text: It works... but is there a better way?
When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 5 with extended axis (0-80, 17 tick marks). All four bars visible, axis highlighted to emphasize crowding.

steps.text: Scale of 5 works, but that's a lot of tick marks. Let's try a bigger scale.
Click 'Scale of 10' to see what happens.

params.description: Preview regenerates with Scale of 10. All four bars fit cleanly. Checkmark indicator appears. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks.

steps.text: Look at that—only 9 tick marks now instead of 17. Much cleaner!
All the data fits, and the graph is easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10. Vertical bar graph with all four bars fitting cleanly. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 (9 tick marks). Checkmark indicator visible.
Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80.
Scale Preview System interactive, Scale of 5 currently showing with crowded axis. Scale of 10 button available.

params.description: Largest value (80) highlighted in data table.

steps.text: Here's the first thing to check: What's your biggest number? That tells you what scales might work.
What's the biggest number in this data?
80 is the biggest. Always check the biggest number first—does it fit on the scale without too many lines on the axis?
You saw that Scale of 5 needed 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 9. For big data, bigger scales make cleaner graphs.
When more than one scale fits, pick the one that's easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80. Value 80 highlighted.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10. Vertical bar graph with all four bars visible. Axis shows 0-80 with 9 tick marks.
Data table showing Books Read This Month: Aisha 20, Ben 35, Carlos 55, Dana 80. Value 80 highlighted.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10. Vertical bar graph with all four bars fitting cleanly.

steps.text: You learned to check if the biggest number fits. But there's something else to check...

params.description: Horizontal data table appears showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System with all four scale buttons visible. Horizontal bar graph format. No graph showing initially.

steps.text: Here's data about marbles. Look at the biggest number—23. That's pretty small!
This graph is horizontal—the bars go sideways. Let's check: which scales fit? Try clicking each scale.
Click each scale to see if 23 fits.
All four scales fit! Let's look at Scale of 1.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing all four scales with checkmarks (all fit). Horizontal orientation.
Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System with all four scale buttons available. Horizontal bar graph format. No graph displayed yet.

steps.text: Click 'Scale of 1' to see what it looks like.

params.description: Preview shows all bars fit with checkmark. Horizontal axis displays 24 tick marks (0-23).

steps.text: It works—every number lands exactly. But look at all those lines on the side!
Small data means all scales fit. But some are cleaner than others.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 1. Horizontal bar graph with all bars fitting (checkmark visible). Axis shows 24 tick marks (0-23), visibly crowded.
Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing all four scales with checkmarks. Scale of 1 button available for selection.

steps.text: Scale of 1 works, but that's a lot of lines. You learned bigger scales are usually easier to read.
Let's try Scale of 10.
Click 'Scale of 10' to compare.

params.description: Preview regenerates with Scale of 10. Bars appear with warning indicators. Jar A (7) between 0 and 10, Jar C (19) between 10 and 20. Only 3 tick marks on axis (0, 10, 20).

steps.text: Hmm. Scale of 10 fits... but there are only 3 marks on the axis. Look at those warnings. Can you tell the exact value of where those bars end? Someone looking at our graph without the data table would need to guess how many marbles are shown.

params.description: Warning indicator on Jar A highlighted with message: '7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle.'

steps.text: For big data, we'd pick Scale of 10 anyway—it's the easiest to read because we don't want too many lines on the axis. But these numbers are small and it's hard to find the exact location of the bar on the graph. We can do better.
When data is small, you have room to find a scale where everything lands exactly.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10. Horizontal bar graph with warning indicators on Jar A (7) and Jar C (19). Axis shows only 0, 10, 20. Jar A warning highlighted.
Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 1 with 24 tick marks. Scale of 10 button available.

steps.text: Let's try a smaller scale. Try a Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2' to preview.

params.description: Preview regenerates with Scale of 2. All bars land exactly. Checkmark indicator appears. No warnings.

steps.text: Look—we can read the end of the bars clearly. All the numbers land exactly. 7 is right at 7, exactly halfway between 6 and 8. 12 is right at 12.
With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1. So you can show any whole number exactly.
Scale of 2 works to show the exact value for all numbers—and for small data, the graph won't have too many marks on the axis.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 2. Horizontal bar graph with all bars landing exactly. Checkmark visible. Axis shows even increments with all values precisely represented.
Data table showing Marbles in Jars: Jar A 7, Jar B 12, Jar C 19, Jar D 23.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10 with warning indicators. Scale of 2 button available.

params.description: Left side: Data Set A showing values 20, 35, 55, 80 with label 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Right side: Data Set B showing values 7, 12, 19, 23 with label 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

steps.text: Here's a quick way to know if you need Scale of 2. Check the last digit of each number in the ones place.
Numbers that end in 0 or 5—like 20, 35, 55, 80—those are multiples of 5 and can be shown clearly with Scales of 5 and 10.

But look at 7, 12, 19, 23. See the digits in the ones place? 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that tells you they aren't multiples of 5 so Scale of 2 might be your best choice. Check our groups. Which data set has last digits that are not 0 or 5?
Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student clicked Data Set B (7, 12, 19, 23), correct

params.description: Data Set B highlighted as correct selection.

steps.text: Right. Look at those last digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5—that's your Scale of 2 signal.

elements.description: Data Set A showing values 20, 35, 55, 80 with label 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B showing values 7, 12, 19, 23 with label 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'. Highlighted as correct selection.
Data Set A showing values 20, 35, 55, 80 with label 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B showing values 7, 12, 19, 23 with label 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Points Scored: Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

steps.text: Here's data from a game. Four rounds of points: 22, 15, 8, 31.
Check the ones place digits: 2, 5, 8, 1. Do they all end in 0 or 5?
22... no. 15... yes! 8... no. 31... no. Three of them don't so they aren't multiples of 5. Which scale will show all values exactly?
Which scale will show ALL values exactly?

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears showing Points Scored with Scale of 2. All bars fit precisely.

steps.text: Scale of 2. All of the bars fit well on the graph and their exact values are clear.

elements.description: Data table showing Points Scored: Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.
Vertical bar graph showing Points Scored with Scale of 2. All bars landing precisely.
Data table showing Points Scored: Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

params.description: Vertical bar graph with Scale of 1 appears alongside Scale of 2 graph for comparison. Same height bars, only axis markings differ.

steps.text: Scale of 1 also shows the exact values, but it would require a lot of marks on the axis to get to the greatest value of 31. Let's consider when we might choose a scale of 1.

elements.description: Data table showing Points Scored: Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.
Vertical bar graph showing Points Scored with Scale of 2.
Vertical bar graph showing Points Scored with Scale of 1. Displayed side-by-side with Scale of 2 graph for comparison.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System available. Vertical bar graph format. No bars showing initially.

steps.text: Look at these numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They're really small!
What happens if we try Scale of 10?
Click 'Scale of 10'

params.description: Preview displays with Scale of 10. Only tick marks at 0 and 10. Bars appear with warning indicators.

steps.text: The bars fit, but we don't know the exact value for any of the bars.

elements.description: Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10. Vertical bar graph with only 0 and 10 tick marks. All bars have warning indicators.
Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System available with vertical bar graph format. No bars displayed yet.

steps.text: Think about the numbers: 9, 5, 7, 3. They don't end in 0 or 5 so we know Scale of 2 is clearer to show any number. Click 'Scale of 2.'
Click 'Scale of 2'

params.description: Preview displays with Scale of 2. All bars show checkmark indicator. Clean graph with 0-10 range.

steps.text: That's better! We can see the bars end at lines or exactly halfway. Scale of 2 could work. But when numbers are this small, you have another option. Try Scale of 1. What do you notice?

elements.description: Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 2. Vertical bar graph with all bars landing precisely. Checkmark visible.
Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 10 with warning indicators. Scale of 2 button available.

steps.text: Click 'Scale of 1'

params.description: Preview displays with Scale of 1. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Checkmark indicator. Clean graph with 0-10 range.

steps.text: Every number lands right on a line. No halfway bars needed. We can read the graph accurately without having the data table.
For very small numbers, Scale of 1 can be a great choice—simple and exact.

elements.description: Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 1. Vertical bar graph with all bars landing exactly on tick marks. Checkmark visible. Clean 0-10 range.
Data table showing Stickers Earned Today: Mia 9, Noah 5, Olivia 7, Pete 3.
Scale Preview System showing Scale of 2. Scale of 1 button available.

steps.text: You know how to check if numbers fit, and when smaller scales are the best choice. Now you'll choose scales on your own.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Favorite Fruits: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Scale Preview System available showing horizontal bar graph format. Scales 2, 5, and 10 available.

steps.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis—that's too much!
Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?
Which scale will you use?
Good choice.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Fruits: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Scale Preview System showing horizontal bar graph format with Scales 2, 5, and 10 available.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Scale Preview System available. Horizontal bar graph format. Scales 2, 5, and 10 selectable.

steps.text: Why is Scale of 10 a good choice? Choose all of the reasons.
options: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

steps.text: Exactly. All values are multiples of 5, so scales of 5 and 10 both work to read the graph clearly. For data going up to 60, Scale of 10 gives you a cleaner graph.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Fruits: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Scale Preview System showing horizontal bar graph format with Scales 2, 5, and 10 available.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Scale Preview System showing horizontal bar graph format with Scales 2, 5, and 10 available.

params.description: Vertical data table appears showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

steps.text: Here's data about how many minutes students practiced their instruments.
Check: are these numbers multiples of 5? Which scale is best for this data?
Which scale is best for this data?
Scale of 2. You saw that 14, 27, 33, 41 aren't multiples of 5 so we need scale of 2 to graph them precisely. Scale of 2 can work when the largest value is 41.

elements.description: Data table showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.
Data table showing Minutes of Practice: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

steps.text: Now create the bar graph.
Set bar heights for all 4 categories.

validator.description: Student set all bar heights correctly

params.description: All four bars correctly placed and locked in position.

steps.text: You chose the right scale and created the graph. Every bar lands exactly where it should.

params.description: Data table appears showing Team Scores: Red Team 25, Green Team 55, Yellow Team 70, Blue Team 40.
Scale Preview System available showing horizontal bar graph format.

steps.text: Last one! Here are scores from four teams. Yellow Team won with 70 points.
You've done this vertical and horizontal now. Same process every time: check the numbers, choose the scale.
Which scale works best for this data?
Good choice. All values end in 0 or 5, and the biggest is 70. Scale of 10 gives you a clean graph.

steps.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number—does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits—are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, Scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.
Let's see what you know.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores: Red Team 25, Green Team 55, Yellow Team 70, Blue Team 40.
Horizontal bar graph with Scale of 10. All four bars complete: Red Team 25, Blue Team 40, Green Team 55, Yellow Team 70.
